_comment: Entries sampled from example.dicts/dict-en-en-2025 — 3 per phrase length category, plus one 15-word entry. Definitions are plain-text extracted from the HTML source.

entries.headword: a aa
entries.definition: Noun. Alternative form of aa.

entries.headword: for you
entries.definition: Preposition. Used as an intensifier to emphasize typicality of behavior.

entries.headword: pork scratching
entries.definition: Noun. (British) A snack food, normally available in pubs, made from cured pork skin that has been salted and deep-fried.

entries.headword: a bene placito
entries.definition: Adverb. (music) At pleasure; ad libitum. Borrowed from Italian a bene placito ("at pleasure").

entries.headword: gentle murder paradox
entries.definition: Noun. A paradox of deontic logic, whereby the plausible statements (i) if you murder, you ought to murder gently, (ii) you do commit murder, and (iii) to murder gently you must murder, imply the less plausible statement (iv) you ought to murder.

entries.headword: pilot not flying
entries.definition: Noun. Synonym of pilot monitoring.

entries.headword: a bob each way
entries.definition: Noun. (colloquial, UK, Australia, New Zealand) A situation of hedging one's bets, refusing to commit to either side of a question.

entries.headword: give someone the shits
entries.definition: Verb. (Australia, colloquial, vulgar) To annoy or frustrate someone to a great degree.

entries.headword: point the finger at
entries.definition: Verb. (transitive, idiomatic) To accuse or blame (someone).

entries.headword: have a good time
entries.definition: Verb. (informal) To enjoy oneself; to have fun. Compare: have a whale of a time.

entries.headword: a blessing and a curse
entries.definition: Noun. Something that is simultaneously a benefit and a burden, or that may seem initially beneficial but also brings unforeseen negative consequences.

entries.headword: go all around the houses
entries.definition: Verb. Alternative form of go around the houses.

entries.headword: piss something up the wall
entries.definition: Verb. (slang, vulgar) To waste or squander (money, talent, opportunity, etc.).

entries.headword: a sheet in the wind's eye
entries.definition: Adjective. Drunk. See three sheets to the wind.

entries.headword: have a whale of a time
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic) To enjoy oneself greatly; to have a very good time.

entries.headword: pull the ladder up behind oneself
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic) To prevent others from attaining or benefiting from the same advantages, opportunities, or rights as oneself.

entries.headword: a day late and a dollar short
entries.definition: Adjective. (US, idiomatic) Too late and too feeble to achieve the desired effect.

entries.headword: have one's work cut out for one
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic) To face a large task or project.

entries.headword: person having ordinary skill in the art
entries.definition: Noun. (UK, law) A hypothetical person having the normal skills and knowledge in a particular technical field, without being a genius; used in a legal test related to patents.

entries.headword: a few roos loose in the top paddock
entries.definition: Noun. (Australia, slang) Alternative form of a kangaroo loose in the top paddock.

entries.headword: go over as well as a lead balloon
entries.definition: Verb. Alternative form of go over like a lead balloon.

entries.headword: march to the beat of a different drum
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic) To do things in one's own way regardless of societal norms and conventional expectations.

entries.headword: close the barn door after the horse has bolted
entries.definition: Verb. Synonym of close the stable door after the horse has bolted.

entries.headword: let the perfect be the enemy of the good
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic) To insist on the total realization of a goal and reject any compromise, thereby decreasing the chance of achieving even a part of that goal.

entries.headword: shut the stable door after the horse has bolted
entries.definition: Verb. Synonym of close the stable door after the horse has bolted.

entries.headword: call a spade a spade and a shovel a shovel
entries.definition: Verb. Alternative form of call a spade a spade.

entries.headword: fall out of the ugly tree and hit every branch
entries.definition: Verb. (slang, derogatory, idiomatic) Of a person: to become extremely unattractive.

entries.headword: have the world by the tail on a downhill pull
entries.definition: Verb. Alternative form of have the world by the tail.

entries.headword: look as if one had lost a shilling and found sixpence
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic, UK) To look very disappointed. In pre-decimal British money, a sixpence was worth half a shilling.

entries.headword: look as if one has been dragged through a hedge backwards
entries.definition: Verb. (intransitive, British, informal, humorous) To look extremely dishevelled.

entries.headword: look as if one has lost a shilling and found sixpence
entries.definition: Verb. (idiomatic, dated) To look annoyed or displeased. A shilling was worth 12 pence, so this would represent a loss of money.

entries.headword: know the difference between one's ass and a hole in the ground
entries.definition: Verb. Alternative form of know one's ass from a hole in the ground.

entries.headword: nervous as a long-tailed cat in a room full of rocking chairs
entries.definition: Adjective. (simile) Extremely nervous. A more elaborate form of nervous as a cat; the implication is that the cat is afraid of its long tail being caught underneath a rocker.

entries.headword: trust someone as far as one could fling a bull by the tail
entries.definition: Verb. Synonym of trust someone as far as one could throw them.

entries._comment: --- 15 words (one of three entries at this length) ---
entries.headword: if it looks, swims, walks and quacks like a duck, then it's probably a duck
entries.definition: Noun. Alternative form of if it looks like a duck, swims like a duck, and quacks like a duck, then it probably is a duck.

_comment_synonyms: One synonym per word-count group, pointing to one entry in that group.
